prescribing_info.title: OZEMPIC (semaglutide) Prescribing Information
prescribing_info.content:
OZEMPIC (SEMAGLUTIDE) INJECTION - FULL PRESCRIBING INFORMATION

MANUFACTURER: Novo Nordisk

BLACK BOX WARNING:
THYROID C-CELL TUMORS
- In rodents, semaglutide causes thyroid C-cell tumors at clinically relevant exposures
- Unknown if causes thyroid tumors in humans
- Contraindicated in patients with personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma (MTC)
- Contraindicated in patients with Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia syndrome type 2 (MEN 2)

MECHANISM: GLP-1 receptor agonist

INDICATIONS:
- Type 2 diabetes mellitus (adjunct to diet and exercise)
- Reduction of major adverse cardiovascular events in T2DM with established CV disease

DOSAGE AND ADMINISTRATION:

Dose Escalation Schedule:
- Week 1-4: 0.25 mg subcutaneously once weekly
- Week 5-8: 0.5 mg subcutaneously once weekly
- Week 9+: May increase to 1 mg once weekly if additional glycemic control needed
- After at least 4 weeks on 1 mg: May increase to 2 mg once weekly

DOSE OPTIONS:
- 0.25 mg weekly (initiation only)
- 0.5 mg weekly (maintenance)
- 1 mg weekly (maintenance)
- 2 mg weekly (maximum)

MAXIMUM DOSE:
- 2 mg subcutaneously once weekly
- Do not exceed 2 mg per week

ADMINISTRATION:
- Subcutaneous injection in abdomen, thigh, or upper arm
- Any time of day, with or without meals
- Same day each week
- May change day if last dose was >2 days ago
- Rotate injection sites within same body region
- If dose missed and <5 days: Give as soon as possible
- If dose missed and >5 days: Skip and give next dose on regular day

RENAL IMPAIRMENT:
- Mild, moderate, severe: No dose adjustment
- ESRD: Limited experience, use with caution
- Monitor renal function if GI adverse reactions occur

HEPATIC IMPAIRMENT:
- No dose adjustment required

CONTRAINDICATIONS:
- Personal or family history of MTC
- MEN 2 syndrome
- Hypersensitivity to semaglutide or any excipient

WARNINGS AND PRECAUTIONS:

Pancreatitis:
- Acute pancreatitis observed
- Discontinue if suspected
- Do not restart if confirmed

Diabetic Retinopathy Complications:
- Rapid improvement in glucose may worsen retinopathy
- Monitor patients with history of retinopathy

Hypoglycemia:
- Risk when used with insulin or sulfonylureas
- May need to reduce insulin/sulfonylurea dose

Acute Kidney Injury:
- Reported, often in patients with dehydration from GI side effects
- Monitor renal function, ensure adequate hydration

Hypersensitivity:
- Anaphylaxis and angioedema reported
- Discontinue and treat promptly

Acute Gallbladder Disease:
- Cholelithiasis and cholecystitis reported
- Monitor for signs/symptoms

DRUG INTERACTIONS:
- Insulin and insulin secretagogues: Increased hypoglycemia risk
- Oral medications: Delays gastric emptying; may affect absorption

ADVERSE REACTIONS (≥5%):
- Nausea (20%)
- Vomiting (9%)
- Diarrhea (9%)
- Abdominal pain (7%)
- Constipation (5%)
- Decreased appetite
- Injection site reactions

PEN DEVICES:
- 0.25 mg or 0.5 mg per dose: Use 2 mg/1.5 mL pen
- 1 mg per dose: Use 4 mg/3 mL pen
- 2 mg per dose: Use 8 mg/3 mL pen
- Each pen provides 4 doses
- Use new needle for each injection
- Do not share pens between patients

STORAGE:
- Before first use: Refrigerate 2-8°C (36-46°F)
- After first use: Store at room temperature (59-86°F) or refrigerated for up to 56 days
- Protect from light
- Do not freeze
- Discard 56 days after first use

HOW SUPPLIED:
- 2 mg/1.5 mL prefilled pen (0.25 or 0.5 mg doses)
- 4 mg/3 mL prefilled pen (1 mg doses)
- 8 mg/3 mL prefilled pen (2 mg doses)

SOURCE PROVENANCE:
This document summarizes key information from the FDA-approved prescribing information.
FDA Application: NDA 209637
Label Revision: 01/2024
For complete prescribing information, refer to the official FDA label at: https://dailymed.nlm.nih.gov/dailymed/drugInfo.cfm?setid=adec4fd2-6858-4c99-91d4-531f5f2a2d79

prescribing_info.title: JARDIANCE (empagliflozin) Prescribing Information
prescribing_info.content:
JARDIANCE (EMPAGLIFLOZIN) - FULL PRESCRIBING INFORMATION

MANUFACTURER: Boehringer Ingelheim/Eli Lilly

MECHANISM: Sodium-glucose co-transporter 2 (SGLT2) inhibitor

INDICATIONS:
- Type 2 diabetes mellitus (adjunct to diet and exercise)
- Heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (regardless of diabetes)
- Heart failure with preserved ejection fraction (regardless of diabetes)
- Reduction of cardiovascular death risk in T2DM with CV disease
- Chronic kidney disease at risk of progression (regardless of diabetes)

DOSAGE - TYPE 2 DIABETES:
- Initial: 10 mg once daily in the morning
- May increase to 25 mg once daily if additional glycemic control needed
- MAXIMUM: 25 mg once daily

DOSAGE - HEART FAILURE (HFrEF and HFpEF):
- Recommended dose: 10 mg once daily
- MAXIMUM: 10 mg once daily

DOSAGE - CHRONIC KIDNEY DISEASE:
- Recommended dose: 10 mg once daily
- MAXIMUM: 10 mg once daily

RENAL DOSING:

For Glycemic Control:
- eGFR ≥30: No dose adjustment
- eGFR <30: Not recommended for glycemic control
- Dialysis: Not recommended

For Heart Failure/CKD:
- eGFR ≥20: 10 mg once daily
- eGFR <20: Not recommended for initiation
- If eGFR falls <20 while on therapy: May continue
- Dialysis: Not recommended

HEPATIC IMPAIRMENT:
- Mild/Moderate: No adjustment
- Severe: Not studied, not recommended

ADMINISTRATION:
- Once daily in the morning
- With or without food
- If missed, take as soon as remembered; do not double next dose

CONTRAINDICATIONS:
- History of serious hypersensitivity to empagliflozin
- Patients on dialysis

WARNINGS AND PRECAUTIONS:

Ketoacidosis:
- DKA occurred in patients with T1DM and T2DM
- May present with normal or near-normal glucose
- Hold before surgery (at least 3 days before)
- Assess for ketoacidosis if nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, malaise, dyspnea

Volume Depletion:
- Can cause symptomatic hypotension
- Assess volume status before initiation
- Monitor in patients with renal impairment, elderly, on diuretics

Urosepsis and Pyelonephritis:
- Serious UTIs requiring hospitalization reported
- Evaluate for signs/symptoms, treat promptly

Hypoglycemia:
- Risk with insulin or insulin secretagogues
- May require lower dose of concomitant agents

Necrotizing Fasciitis (Fournier's Gangrene):
- Rare but serious necrotizing infection of perineum
- Discontinue and treat urgently

Genital Mycotic Infections:
- Increased risk of genital yeast infections
- Monitor and treat as appropriate

Lower Limb Amputation:
- Increased risk observed in some SGLT2 inhibitors
- Monitor for new ulcers, wounds, skin infections

DRUG INTERACTIONS:
- Diuretics: Increased volume depletion risk
- Insulin/secretagogues: Hypoglycemia risk, may need dose reduction
- Positive urine glucose tests: Interferes with 1,5-AG assay

ADVERSE REACTIONS:
- Urinary tract infection (9%)
- Female genital mycotic infection (5%)
- Upper respiratory infection (3%)
- Increased urination (3%)
- Male genital mycotic infection (3%)
- Nausea
- Thirst
- Hypotension

SURGERY:
- Discontinue at least 3 days before scheduled surgery
- May restart when oral intake resumed and patient clinically stable
- Risk of perioperative DKA

HOW SUPPLIED:
- 10 mg tablets
- 25 mg tablets

SOURCE PROVENANCE:
This document summarizes key information from the FDA-approved prescribing information.
FDA Application: NDA 204629
Label Revision: 12/2023
For complete prescribing information, refer to the official FDA label at: https://dailymed.nlm.nih.gov/dailymed/drugInfo.cfm?setid=fbc32a9e-7e55-4f1b-b6d3-0e66c08d4d6c

prescribing_info.title: LANTUS (insulin glargine) Prescribing Information
prescribing_info.content:
LANTUS (INSULIN GLARGINE) INJECTION - FULL PRESCRIBING INFORMATION

MANUFACTURER: Sanofi

WARNINGS:
- NEVER administer intravenously or via insulin pump
- Do NOT dilute or mix with other insulins
- Hypoglycemia is most common adverse effect

MECHANISM: Long-acting basal insulin analog; forms microprecipitates allowing slow, prolonged absorption

INDICATIONS:
- Type 1 diabetes (adults and pediatric patients ≥6 years)
- Type 2 diabetes (adults)

DOSAGE - TYPE 1 DIABETES:
- Individualize dose based on metabolic needs
- Typically 1/3 to 1/2 of total daily insulin as basal
- Initial: ~0.2-0.4 units/kg/day
- Adjust based on fasting glucose, A1C

DOSAGE - TYPE 2 DIABETES:
- Initial: 0.1-0.2 units/kg/day OR 10 units once daily
- Adjust based on fasting glucose
- Target fasting glucose: 80-130 mg/dL typically

TITRATION:
- Adjust every 3-4 days based on fasting glucose
- Increase by 2 units if FG >130 mg/dL
- Increase by 4 units if FG >180 mg/dL
- If hypoglycemia: Reduce dose by 10-20%

MAXIMUM DOSE:
- No absolute maximum; dose individualized
- Doses >100 units/day may be needed in highly insulin-resistant patients
- Consider U-300 Toujeo for high-dose requirements

ADMINISTRATION:
- Subcutaneous injection once daily at same time each day
- May inject in abdomen, thigh, or deltoid
- Rotate injection sites within same body region
- Do NOT inject intravenously or use in insulin pumps
- Do NOT mix with other insulins

DURATION OF ACTION:
- Onset: 1-2 hours
- No pronounced peak
- Duration: Up to 24 hours
- Some patients may require twice-daily dosing

CONVERSION:

From Once-Daily NPH:
- Same unit dose once daily

From Twice-Daily NPH:
- Reduce total daily NPH dose by 20%
- Give as single daily dose

From Other Long-Acting Insulins:
- Generally unit-for-unit conversion
- Monitor closely, adjust as needed

PEDIATRIC DOSING:
- ≥6 years: Same principles as adults
- Individualize based on needs
- Total daily insulin: ~0.5-1 unit/kg/day typically

RENAL IMPAIRMENT:
- May require reduced doses
- Monitor glucose closely
- Insulin clearance decreased in renal failure

HEPATIC IMPAIRMENT:
- May require reduced doses
- Monitor glucose closely
- Insulin clearance may be decreased

CONTRAINDICATIONS:
- During hypoglycemic episodes
- Hypersensitivity to insulin glargine or excipients

WARNINGS:
- Hypoglycemia: Most common adverse reaction; risk with changes in regimen, food intake, exercise, renal/hepatic function
- Hypokalemia: Monitor potassium, especially with potassium-lowering drugs
- Fluid retention with concomitant thiazolidinediones
- Hypersensitivity reactions
- Medication errors: Verify insulin type before administration

DRUG INTERACTIONS:
- Drugs increasing hypoglycemia risk: ACE inhibitors, salicylates, MAOIs, fluoxetine, pentoxifylline
- Drugs decreasing effect: Corticosteroids, diuretics, glucagon, growth hormone, thyroid hormones
- Beta-blockers: May mask hypoglycemia symptoms

ADVERSE REACTIONS:
- Hypoglycemia (most common)
- Injection site reactions
- Lipodystrophy
- Pruritus, rash
- Weight gain
- Allergic reactions (rare)

STORAGE:
- Unopened: Refrigerate 2-8°C (36-46°F); do not freeze
- In-use (opened): Room temperature <30°C (86°F) for up to 28 days
- Protect from light and heat
- Discard 28 days after opening

HOW SUPPLIED:
- 100 units/mL in:
  - 10 mL vials
  - 3 mL SoloStar pen (5 pens per box)
- Each SoloStar pen delivers 1-80 units per injection

SOURCE PROVENANCE:
This document summarizes key information from the FDA-approved prescribing information.
FDA Application: BLA 021081
Label Revision: 10/2023
For complete prescribing information, refer to the official FDA label at: https://dailymed.nlm.nih.gov/dailymed/drugInfo.cfm?setid=0d27a6e2-6a9c-49d1-9c1c-d58a9c0a8d75
